senses.definition: a three-stringed Japanese musical instrument played with a plectrum, producing a bright, nasal tone
examples: She learned to play the samisen for the traditional festival.
The samisen accompanied the singer with its distinct, shimmering sound.

senses.definition: plural of samisen; multiple three-stringed Japanese musical instruments
examples: The ensemble included three samisens and a shamisen.
Collectors sought out antique samisens from the Meiji era.

senses.definition: in a state of scorn or bad temper; not polite or friendly
examples: He gave a samish reply to the question.
The crowd grew samish after the announcements.

senses.definition: a heavy, rich fabric used for clothing in medieval times; also a material similar to silk or satin
examples: The knight wore a samite cloak over his armor.
Her gown was stitched from luxurious samite.

senses.definition: plural of samite; medieval fabrics
examples: The judges wore garments of samites and velvets.
Archaeologists found bolts of samites in the monastery storehouse.

senses.definition: a term used in Indian political or social contexts for a council or committee; often relates to a gathering or delegation
examples: The samithi met to discuss local development projects.
A samithi was formed to address community issues.

senses.definition: plural of samithi; multiple councils or committees
examples: Several samithis were formed in different wards.
The samithis collaborated on the festival planning.

senses.definition: a council or committee in Indian contexts; variant spelling of samithi
examples: The samiti approved the proposal after review.
A cultural samiti organized the event.

senses.definition: plural of samiti; multiple councils or committees
examples: Different samitis were responsible for various festival tasks.
Members from the samitis attended the conference.

senses.definition: see samite above; obligation to avoid repetition
examples: Refer to samite for context.

senses.definition: see samites above; repeat placeholder
examples: Refer to samites for context.

senses.definition: see samithi above
examples: Refer to samithi for context.

senses.definition: see samitis above
examples: Refer to samitis for context.

senses.definition: a clandestine, self-published, or underground press publication, especially of censored or banned material
examples: The samizdat circulated quietly among dissidents.
Scholarship on samizdat reveals how readers shared banned texts.

senses.definition: plural of samizdat; underground publications
examples: Copies of samizdats were passed hand to hand.
Researchers study the spread of samizdats in secret networks.

senses.definition: Sanskrit term for a ritual or spiritual practice, often used in Indian philosophy and Hinduism; variant transliteration of Sankara (Shankaracharya name) or a ritual concept depending on context
examples: He studied samkara as part of his philosophy course.
The guru spoke about samkara and its role in purification.

senses.definition: an ancient Indian philosophy that explains reality through two fundamental realities: purusha and prakriti
examples: Samkhya is one of the six orthodox schools of Indian philosophy.
The teacher compared yoga concepts with Samkhya ideas.

senses.definition: an old or dialect word meaning a small measure or quantity; historically used in some Scandinavian languages
examples: A samle trowe (archaic usage) was used in recipes.

senses.definition: plural of samlet; archaic or dialect noun meaning small measures
examples: The collection included several samlets.

senses.definition: a horse-drawn cart or light vehicle used in parts of Asia; also spelled 'samloar' in some dialects
examples: They traveled by samlor along the river road.
The village market was reached by a wooden samlor.

senses.definition: plural of samlor; multiple carts or wagons
examples: The market brought many samlors into the square.

senses.definition: past tense of 'sam' as a verb meaning to dominate or to best in a casual, slang sense; or a possible past tense of a dialect verb; usage is highly limited and regional
examples: They sammed the rival team in the semifinal.
In older texts, he sammed his opponents in mock combat.

senses.definition: to collect or gather together; historically used in Germanic languages; modern usage is rare
examples: They sammel coins from the old chest.
Archivists sammel several manuscripts for the exhibit.

senses.definition: a person who sammels; or a person who carbo- imparts; usage is rare
examples: The sammer organized the artifacts.

senses.definition: comparative form of 'sammie' or 'samm' in dialect; meaning more stuffed or more crowded; usage extremely limited
examples: The bakery was sammier than yesterday.

senses.definition: slang for sandwiches; common in American English
examples: I packed sammies for the picnic.
She bought three sammies at the deli.

senses.definition: present participle of 'samm' with uncertain meaning; possibly a dialect term meaning to tease or to squeeze; not common in standard English
examples: They were samm-ing the ropes to test tension.

senses.definition: a name or nickname; also short for Samuel; can refer to a sausage sandwich in some regions
examples: Sammy is coming to the party.
He calls his brother Sammy as a nickname.

senses.definition: informal term for a hot dog or sausage sandwich in some dialects
examples: I grabbed a quick Sammy for lunch.

senses.definition: likely a transliteration of a proper noun from a South Asian or Middle Eastern language; may refer to a family name or a religious title; usage is highly context-dependent
examples: The scholar attended as Dr. Samnani.
The Samnani family hosted the event.
